coredata.dc:title: Rapid synthesis of functional carbon dots by microwave: Advanced information security anti-counterfeiting material
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ning
Fu, Qiang
Sun, Shouhong
Lu, Kangzhi
Dong, Zhanhua
Yue, Mingbo
coredata.dc:description: Since carbon dots (CDs) exhibit exceptional optical qualities, they have been the focus of much research. Luminescent materials based on CDs are increasingly appearing, but the realization of solid-state matrix-free long-lasting afterglow and white fluorescence remains challenging. In this work, a microwave-assisted synthesis route is used to prepare solid-state white fluorescent CDs with 6 s matrix-free dual (503 nm, 542 nm) delayed fluorescence (DF) afterglow that can be observed by naked eye which uses 1,2,4,5-benzenetetracarboxylic acid (BA) and ethanol as precursors. And polychromatic nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) are prepared using tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) as nitrogen source, and the solvent controlled synthesis is typical. The solid-state green N-CDs prepared from ethanol (E-CDs) and white fluorescence N-CDs prepared from deionized water (W-CDs) have 3.5 s and 1.2 s (527 nm, 558 nm) visible matrix-free room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) afterglow, respectively. Our work has found (n, π*) structure providing a new strategy for developing CDs as solid-state long-lasting matrix-free afterglow materials and a new reference value for their potential applications in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-state white fluorescence
Matrix-free afterglow
Nitrogen dope carbon dots
Microwave

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of E-CDs, W-CDs, F-CDs, and D-CDs (Route 2)
Preparation and formation mechanism of CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Youth Talent Program Startup Foundation of Qufu Normal University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the Youth Talent Program Startup Foundation of Qufu Normal University (No. 602601), the Natural Science Foundation of Rizhao (No. RZ2021ZR37) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (No. ZR2022MB047).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and particles size distribution images of (a) P-CDs (b) E-CDs (c) W-CDs (d) F-CDs and (e) D-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs solution (0.25 g/mL) under 530 nm (P-CDs, E-CDs, W-CDs, F-CDs and D-CDs). Fluorescence spectra of (b) E-CDs, (c) W-CDs, (d) F-CDs, (e) D-CDs solution (0.25 g/mL) at different excitation wavelengths (370 nm-550 nm) (f) Visible fluorescence of E-CDs, W-CDs, F-CDs, D-CDs under 365 nm UV-lamp irradiation.

Energy graphs and related transitions of CDs (P stands for P-CDs, E stands for E-CDs, W stands for W-CDs.). The dotted blue arrow line represents a non-radiative transition. The radiant transition from excited state S1 to ground state S0 is shown by green arrows (Fluo), the transition from triplet state T1 (n → π*) to S0 is shown by dark yellow arrows, and the delayed fluorescence from S1 to S0 is shown by gray arrows.
P-CDs ink printed on banknote and paper under (a, d) sunlight, (b, e) ultraviolet irradiation (λex = 360 nm) and (c, f) the corresponding afterglow photos after the UV lamp is turned off.
Synthesis process of (a) P-CDs and (b) E-CDs, W-CDs, F-CDs and D-CDs.
Data analysis of Raman results of P-CDs, E-CDs, W-CDs, F-CDs and D-CDs.
Data analysis of XPS N1s spectra of E-CDs, W-CDs, F-CDs and D-CDs.

head.ce:title: Rapid synthesis of functional carbon dots by microwave: Advanced information security anti-counterfeiting material

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Rizhao, Shandong 276826, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Rizhao, Shandong 276826, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since carbon dots (CDs) exhibit exceptional optical qualities, they have been the focus of much research. Luminescent materials based on CDs are increasingly appearing, but the realization of solid-state matrix-free long-lasting afterglow and white fluorescence remains challenging. In this work, a microwave-assisted synthesis route is used to prepare solid-state white fluorescent CDs with 6 s matrix-free dual (503 nm, 542 nm) delayed fluorescence (DF) afterglow that can be observed by naked eye which uses 1,2,4,5-benzenetetracarboxylic acid (BA) and ethanol as precursors. And polychromatic nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) are prepared using tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) as nitrogen source, and the solvent controlled synthesis is typical. The solid-state green N-CDs prepared from ethanol (E-CDs) and white fluorescence N-CDs prepared from deionized water (W-CDs) have 3.5 s and 1.2 s (527 nm, 558 nm) visible matrix-free room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) afterglow, respectively. Our work has found (n, π*) structure providing a new strategy for developing CDs as solid-state long-lasting matrix-free afterglow materials and a new reference value for their potential applications in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: CDs, as an emerging zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, have attracted attention due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, optical stability, easy availability, and low cost of precursor materials [1-3]. Therefore, based on these featuring advantages, CDs have also become an excellent candidate for luminescent materials, which have bypassed the defects of traditional inorganic and metallic compounds as luminescent materials in certain ways [4]. CDs-based luminescent materials have outstanding potential in the fields of anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, photoelectric devices, biological probes, optical sensing [5-8], etc. Lou et al. realized a new type of CDs material with thermal activation delayed afterglow (TADF), providing new ideas for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption [9]. Ding et al. prepared CDs with long-lived RTP by a microwaved-assisted and prospected their applications in labeling and information encryption [10]. Song et al. used the method of hot urea bath CDs to improve the phosphorescence efficiency, which paved the way for a wide range of applications of high-performance CDs phosphorescent materials, from information encryption to lighting to new types of displays [11]. Many studies have shown that CDs is a material with great research potential, and their afterglow characteristics are constantly being explored. Despite this, there are still some obstacles to overcome, such as the existence of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which makes solid fluorescence hard to fulfill, and solid-state white fluorescence is also rare. The most important thing is to accomplish effective afterglow emission because most CDs-based afterglow materials always require matrix, which has certain limitations [12,13]. The solution to these issues will provide beneficial reference values for CD-based luminescent materials.

Here, a microwave-assisted synthesis route is used to prepare solid-state white fluorescent and long-lasting visible 6 s yellow afterglow CDs via BA and ethanol. Interestingly, the addition of TEPA as a nitrogen source, the multi-color emission of N-CDs is realized and has the characteristics of solvent controlled synthesis [14,15]. The presence of special surface functional groups may alleviate the barriers generated by ACQ to realize solid white fluorescence, and there is a special (n, π*) configurational transitions in as-prepared CDs in this work, so that the CDs does not need a matrix to achieve the afterglow. The E-CDs and W-CDs powders have green and white fluorescence, respectively, and have visible yellow and white afterglow of 3.5 s and 1.2 s, respectively. Our work not only realized the CDs solid-state fluorescence, but also achieved long-lasting afterglow based on CDs used as ink, providing a new strategy for developing CDs as anti-counterfeiting and information encryption materials.

Here, we named the preparation experiment of pure CDs (P-CDs) without nitrogen doping as Route 1. First, 125 mg BA was mixed with 2 mL ethanol, and then sonicated for 2 min. The sonicated emulsion mixture was then transferred to microwave oven (700 W) for 20 min on low heat. The resulting P-CDs solution was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at the speed of 3500 rpm/min for 5 min. Then it was purified in 3000-dialysis bag for 16 h and vacuum dried at 80 °C f for 7 h to obtain the powder for characterization. The dried P-CDs powder was dissolved in ethanol with a concentration of 0.25 g/mL (Scheme 1 a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of E-CDs, W-CDs, F-CDs, and D-CDs (Route 2)
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDs experiment prepared with different solvents was named Route 2. Similarly, 125 mg BA and 9.5 μL TEPA were dissolved in ethanol absolute (2 mL), The solution was sonicated for 2 min and turned into a white emulsion, then placed on low heat in a household microwave oven (700 W) for 20 min. The obtained white solid was cooled to room temperature and dissolved in the corresponding solvent absolute ethanol (2 mL), and the supernatant was centrifuged at a low speed for 5 min (3500 rpm/min). The prepared CDs was named E-CDs. W-CDs, F-CDs and D-CDs were prepared by replacing absolute ethanol with deionized water, formamide and N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) in the same way. The N-CDs solution was purified in 3000-dialysis bag for 16 h, and then vacuum dried at 80 °C for 7 h to obtain the CDs powder for characterization. The dried N-CDs powder was dissolved in corresponding solvent with a concentration of 0.25 g/mL (Scheme 1b).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs solution were measured on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were collected on a JEM-2100. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a Nicolet iS20 spectrometer. Raman spectroscopy was performed on a HORIBA LabRAM laser confocal microscopic Raman spectrometer (excited by a 633 nm laser). UV–vis absorption spectra were collected on a Lambda 1050 UV–Visible-near-infrared spectrophotometer. The afterglow lifetime decay curves of three CDs powders and afterglow spectra of P-CDs and W-CDs were monitored on a steady state and transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured with the MinFlex600 X-ray diffractometer at a scanning speed of 20°/min in the range of 10 to 70 degrees at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and formation mechanism of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As described in the experiment section, Route 1 prepared P-CDs by microwave-assisted heating of BA and ethanol. The P-CDs solution appeared blue fluorescence, but the visible afterglow duration of the white P-CDs powder was up to 6 s. Route 2 synthesized N-CDs with different emission wavelengths by microwave-assisted solvent heat treatment of BA and TEPA in different solvents. BA was selected as a carbon source due to its conjugated sp2 structure and carboxyl groups [16], while TEPA was selected as a nitrogen source due to its nitrogen content. The dope of nitrogen can reduce the band gap, synthesize CDs in narrow band gaps, and control the fluorescence red shift [8,17]. During the synthesis process, the precursors not only amide in the solvent environment, but also continuously dehydrate and carbonize under microwave conditions, and finally forms CDs, which are reflected in different degrees of graphitization, doping content and forms, thus forming CDs with different optical properties. The synthesis scheme of E-CDs, W-CDs, F-CDs, and D-CDs is illustrated in Scheme 1.

To further explore the origin of the properties of synthetic CDs, we characterize the morphology by TEM [18-21], as shown in Fig. 1 . The particle size of P-CDs ranges from 1.23 to 3.35 nm, and the mean size of the one hundred P-CDs particles is 2.18 ± 0.06 nm (Fig. 1a). The lattice spacing is about 0.21 nm which can be observed in the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) [22].The particle size of E-CDs ranges from 1.79 nm to 3.45 nm, with an average size of 2.69 ± 0.04 nm which is calculated by measuring fifty E-CDs particles and a lattice spacing about 0.20 nm (Fig. 1b).The particle size distribution of W-CDs ranges from 2.12 nm to 4.95 nm and the average size of the fifty W-CDs particles is 3.54 ± 0.04 nm with a lattice spacing about 0.22 nm (Fig. 1c), while that of F-CDs ranges from 1.06 nm to 8.24 nm, with an average size of one hundred F-CDs particles is 4.67 ± 0.09 nm, and the lattice spacing is approximately 0.17 nm (Fig. 1d). Finally, the size distribution of cyan fluorescent D-CDs ranges from 2.37 nm to 4.75 nm and the average size of the one hundred D-CDs particles is 3.36 ± 0.06 nm and the lattice spacing is about 0.25 nm (Fig. 1e). By observing most of the major particle size distributions of CDs sizes above, we speculate that the long-wavelength emission of CDs may be attributed to π conjugation, which may relate to CDs size [23,24]. Later, we further demonstrate this conjecture by characterizing XPS and other data.

The Raman spectra corresponding to the five CDs are shown in Fig. S1 and Table 1 . Raman spectrum is used to characterize the defect degree of CDs, the relative strength of D band reflects the disorder degree of crystalline structure, and G band is used to characterize the sp2 bond structure of CDs [25,26]. The D and G bands are usually distributed approximately 1340–1400 cm−1 and 1560–1700 cm−1, respectively [27]. By observing Fig. S1 and Table 1, we find that P-CDs, W-CDs, and F-CDs conform to this range, and the D-bands of E-CDs and D-CDs are shifted to more than 1400 cm−1, we speculate that this may be related to surface defects [28,29]. In addition, by observing the ratio of the D-band to G-band, we speculate that the long-wavelength emission of F-CDs is related to its sp2 ordered carbon structure [30,31], so we further characterize XPS of these CDs. As shown in Fig. S2, Fig. S3 and Table 2 , we can find that nitrogen doping makes F-CDs have a relatively high degree of graphitization, and greater π conjugation makes it have long wavelength emission [15]. In addition, the high degree of graphitization makes F-CDs have more N-bond carbon rings, which may also be one of the reasons why the excitation independence of F-CDs is different from the excitation dependence of other CDs in this work [32]. By observing the XPS results of P-CDs and N-CDs, we find that P-CDs contains lots of C=O type (n, π*) configuration (Fig. S2, S3), and W-CDs contains a corresponding C=O/C=N (n, π*) configuration, which may be related to its afterglow emission [11]. The XRD pattern in Fig. S4 shows that P-CDs, E-CDs, W-CDs and D-CDs correspond to graphite (002) planes at ≈25° and F-CDs at ≈29°, where the corresponding lattice plane of P-CDs and E-CDs near 40° is (100) [33-35]. The results of FTIR characterization are shown in Fig. S5. The typical stretching vibration of O-H exists in the range of 3300–3500 cm−1 at all five CDs, and the locations fall to 3450 cm−1 (P-CDs), 3432 cm−1 (E-CDs),3438 cm−1 (W-CDs), 3473 cm−1 (F-CDs), 3435 cm−1 (D-CDs), respectively [36-38]. However, in the range of 3000–3100 cm−1, the P-CDs has no signal peak, while the signal peak of the four N-CDs corresponds to N-H stretching vibration peak of the amino group, indicating the successful incorporation of nitrogen element. In addition, the typical stretching vibration of C=C/C=N exists in 1500–1600 cm−1 indicates the successful completion of the dehydration and oxidation and some sp2 conjugations are replaced by nitrogen, which indicates that the form of aromatic nitrogen is involved in the formation of sp2 hybridization of CDs [39,40]. The oxygen group O-C=O is also found in the interval 1690–1800 cm−1. That means carboxyl is present in the surface of four N-CDs.

The UV absorption spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. S6, further verifying our hypothesis that the electron conjugate structure is the influencing factor of fluorescence [41]. As described in the UV absorption spectrum, P-CDs and E-CDs have absorption peaks at 211 nm corresponds to π → π*, and at 294 nm corresponds to n → π* transitions from the C=O double bond, and the absorption of E-CDs at 302 nm may correspond to C=O and C=N, indicating that fluorescence of CDs comes from the electron sp2 conjugate structure (Fig. S6b, c). This is also consistent with FTIR results. D-CDs detects absorption peaks at 266 nm and 305 nm corresponding to π → π* transitions of C=C and n → π* transitions of types C=O and C=N, respectively. Similarly, UV absorption signals are observed at 249 nm and 328 nm for W-CDs compared to the three CDs described earlier, and a higher ratio of absorption peaks is seen at 328 nm, speculating a relatively high number of n → π* transitions (Fig. S6d). In addition, the absorption peak at 533 nm is also detected for W-CDs, which may be related to the surface state (C=N) transition. Notably, strong absorption signals have been observed at 274 nm and 312 nm for F-CDs, and n → π* transitions corresponding to C=N, speculating that there is more nitrogen doping lead to red shift fluorescence of the CDs (Fig. S6e). Therefore, the results show that the sp2 conjugated structure domain has a significant influence on the photophysical properties of CDs [42,43]. The logical explanation is the conjugation between the CDs skeleton elements. Therefore, we believe that the fluorescence of CDs mainly comes from the electron conjugate domain, and the red shift of fluorescence comes from the doping of nitrogen element.

The emission wavelength of the P-CDs in liquid state is 491 nm, and the fluorescence is enhanced by contrast with the raw material, which further explains the optical properties of CDs (Fig. S7). To study the fluorescence characteristics of N-CDs, the fluorescence spectra of CDs (E-CDs, W-CDs, F-CDs, D-CDs) synthesized by four different solvents are measured (Fig. 2 ). For W-CDs, when the excitation wavelength changes from 370 nm to 550 nm, the emission wavelength is red shifted, and the intensity decreases. When the excitation wavelength is 470 nm, the emission wavelength is red shifted to 612 nm. W-CDs and E-CDs have the same excitation dependent property, and emission can be red shifted to 601 nm under 510 nm excitation. As the excitation wavelength increases from 370 nm to 550 nm, the emission wavelength is stable in the red emission band. Under the excitation of 510 nm, the longest wavelength can reach 691 nm, and the background interference is small. Moreover, the red emission is believed to be able to avoid the self-organizing interference in biomedicine. Long wavelength emission is beneficial for biomedical applications. For D-CDs, it has the same excitation dependence characteristics as E-CDs and W-CDs. With the increase of excitation wavelength from 370 nm to 550 nm, D-CDs shows emission red shift, and the wavelength reaches 629 nm under 510 nm excitation. Therefore, by analyzing the fluorescence spectra, we confirm that the dope of nitrogen successfully synthesizes the long-wavelength emission CDs. In addition, the CDs solutions synthesized by four different solvents under UV irradiation at 365 nm show fluorescence of various colors. Under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp, E-CDs shows blue fluorescence, W-CDs show green fluorescence, D-CDs show cyan fluorescence and F-CDs shows red fluorescence (Figs. 2f, S8). Fig. S9 shows the CIE coordinates corresponding to five CDs (P-CDs and four N-CDs) in the liquid state (CIE 1931), which also demonstrates the effect of nitrogen doping on the optical properties of CDs. The blue fluorescent P-CDs and E-CDs CIE are (0.30, 0.38) and (0.29, 0.36), respectively. The CIE coordinate of blue-green D-CDs is (0.30, 0.36), and the CIE coordinate of green photoluminescent W-CDs is (0.29, 0.36). Solid state fluorescence CIE coordinates of P-CDs and W-CDs correspond to (0.24, 0.34) and (0.31, 0.33), and afterglow coordinates correspond to (0.37, 0.45) and (0.30, 0.32), respectively (Fig. S10). It is worth noting that the CIE coordinate of red F-CDs is (0.60, 0.38), formamide may act as a nitrogen-doped solvent, it also promotes the conversion of CDs emission from cyan to red. This is attributed to the deep doping of nitrogen, which affects the structure of CDs and thus its optical properties. Using Rhodamine 6G as the reference solution, the quantum yields of P-CDs, E-CDs, W-CDs, and D-CDs were 33.6% (P-CDs), 31.9% (E-CDs), 50.3%(W-CDs) and 44.6% (D-CDs), respectively. And the quantum yield of F-CDs calculated with Rhodamine B as the reference solution is 25.8% [44].

It is challenging to realize solid state fluorescence because direct π-π accumulation causes nonradiative transitions [45]. In our work, P-CDs, E-CDs, W-CDs and D-CDs may overcome ACQ by forming surface functional groups to achieve solid-state fluorescence (Fig. S5). We observed the fluorescence of raw materials and P-CDs, E-CDs, W-CDs and D-CDs powder, in Routes 1 and 2 (Fig. S11). Under 365 nm excitation, P-CDs, E-CDs, W-CDs displayed purple (480 nm), green (503 nm), white (445 nm, 550 nm) fluorescence, respectively (Fig. S12b). W-CDs exhibit double emission characteristics, which is consistent with the theory that white is a polychromatic light, perhaps this is the origin of its white fluorescence [11]. The reason for the difference in the color of the CDs in the solid and liquid state may be due to the difference in its structure and environment. In the solid state, the luminescence of the CDs mainly comes from the functional groups on its surface. Different functional groups lead to different electron energy level structures and band structures, which determines the difference in luminous color. In the liquid state, the mechanism of carbon emission is more complex. In the liquid environment, the functional groups on the surface of CDs may undergo structural changes, resulting in the adjustment of their band structure, which affects the luminous color [2]. When the UV disappeared, the afterglow emission of 6 s (P-CDs), 3.5 s (E-CDs), and 1.2 s (W-CDs) can be observed by the naked eye (Fig. S11; Video S1, Supporting Information). Both P-CDs and E-CDs showed green afterglow and W-CDs displayed white afterglow.

The afterglow includes RTP, DF and other optical properties. To explore the type of afterglow of CDs, we typically tested the afterglow emission spectra of P-CDs and W-CDs under excitation at different temperatures of 365 nm (Fig. S14), and finally found that P-CDs showed DF afterglow and W-CDs showed RTP afterglow [9,11]. The afterglow without matrix in this work is aided by the presence of a special (n, π*) structure type transition in CDs. C=O/C=N promotes the generation of afterglow because of its strong spin–orbit coupling (SOC), facilitates the intersystem conversion (ISC) process, develops the formation of triplet excitons, and reduces the energy level difference between singlet and triplet (ΔEST) to enhance afterglow emission [11]. Thus, the possible fluorescence/afterglow mechanism diagram is shown in Fig. 3 . With respect to fluorescence, it can be inferred that two types of transitions, π → π* and n → π*, occur when the CDs is excited to the excited state. When the CDs is excited, the transition from S1 back to S0 produces a radiative transition, which we see as fluorescence, and the other part is lost in the form of a nonradiative transition (dotted blue arrow line).

By observing UV absorption, we know that W-CDs has absorption peak signals at 249 nm and 328 nm, respectively (Fig. S6, S12c, e). (n, π*) configuration may correspond to C=O in P-CDs, controlling P-CDs to go from S1 state through ISC process to T state, and then from T state through RISC process to S1 state to achieve DF afterglow. For W-CDs, the (n, π*) configuration C=O/C=N may only go through the ISC process to return to the T state so that W-CDs can return from the T state to the S0 state to achieve RTP afterglow. Here, we name S1 (π → π*) as from 249 nm to S0 ground-state type, and S1 (n → π*) from 328 nm to S0 type. We speculate that the afterglow mechanism of P-CDs is roughly that when T1 (n → π*) is reached, the (n, π*) configuration corresponding to P-CDs may be C=O to control P-CDs to produce RISC, so that P-CDs returns to the ground state S0 through RISC, thus producing delayed fluorescence. Different from other CDs, the absorption peak at 533 nm is speculated to be the influence of C=N of its surface state [46,47]. It can be seen in Fig. S13d that W-CDs has two emission peak spanning which correspond to 527 nm and 558 nm emission which widely range from 450 nm to 700 nm, thus white fluorescence was created. Therefore, the general principle of afterglow of W-CDs powder is inferred as follows: The absorption of energy at 249 nm and 328 nm excites π → π* transition and n → π* transition. During the transition from S1 (π → π*) and S1 (n → π*) to S0, part of the energy is lost in the form of non-radiation, while the other part is shown in the form of fluorescence. So that's what we see in the white fluorescence of W-CDs [48], where (n, π*) configuration may correspond to C=O/C=N in W-CDs. During the transition from T1 to S0, nonradiative transition and room temperature afterglow occurs simultaneously. The afterglow of P-CDs was as long as 6 s DF afterglow, and E-CDs was as long as 3.5 s RTP afterglow which was observed by the naked eye (Fig. S11).

To realize the application of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption, the P-CDs is printed on Chinese currency and paper as anti-counterfeiting ink (Fig. 4 ), and the words “RM” in “RMB” on the currency and “23” in “2023” on the paper are printed. P-CDs [49]. When illuminated by UV lamp, the word “RMB” on banknote fluoresces white, as does the word "2023” on the paper. However, when the UV lamp is turned off, the words “RM” and “23” printed with P-CDs ink will emit a strong yellow afterglow, and the fluorescence will gradually fade with time. According to the results of decay curves, the afterglow lifetime of the three CDs powders (P-CDs, E-CDs, and W-CDs) are estimated to be 476 ms,292 ms and 259 ms, respectively (Fig. 4g) [50]. In addition, we also printed the ink into partial butterflies on paper, and recorded its afterglow video (Video S2, Supporting information). Similarly, when the UV light is removed, butterflies coated with P-CDs ink show a yellow afterglow. All these practices prove the successful application of this work in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

Using a simple microwave-assisted synthesis, we successfully achieved multi-color emission and afterglow emission of CDs and explored the effect of nitrogen doping on the properties of CDs. The more remarkable achievement is that we explored the possible afterglow mechanism of CDs. The excellent (n, π*) configuration makes the P-CDs and W-CDs separate with the DF and RTP features. And it has been successfully applied to anti-counterfeiting printing and information encryption. This discovery not only has reference value for the study of multicolor emission CDs, but also takes a new step in the field of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

N.L. did experiments, wrote the manuscript, and interpreted the data. S.S. and K.L. were responsible for revising the paper. Z.D. and M.Y. supported the characterizations. Q.F. conceived the experiments, administered and supervised the project and acquired the funding.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the Youth Talent Program Startup Foundation of Qufu Normal University (No. 602601), the Natural Science Foundation of Rizhao (No. RZ2021ZR37) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (No. ZR2022MB047).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.flatc.2023.100587.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Yao, H. Huang, Y. Liu, Z. Kang, Carbon Dots: A Small Conundrum. Trends in Chemistry 1(2) (2019) 235-246.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, Y. Song, X. Zhao, J. Shao, J. Zhang, B. Yang, The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective. Nano Research 8(2) (2015) 355-381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green carbon dots with multifaceted applications– Waste to wealth strategy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S H. Salimi Shahraki, A. Ahmad, R. Bushra,Green carbon dots with multifaceted applications– Waste to wealth strategy. FlatChem, 31 (2022).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis of high-yield red fluorescent carbon dots with ultra-narrow emission by controlled O/N elements

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xian, K. Li, Hydrothermal Synthesis of High-Yield Red Fluorescent Carbon Dots with Ultra-Narrow Emission by Controlled O/N Elements. Advanced Materials 34(21) (2022) e2201031.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green and near-infrared dual-mode afterglow of carbon dots and their applications for confidential information readout

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, K. Jiang, J. Du, L. Zheng, Y. Li, Z. Li, H. Lin, Green and Near-Infrared Dual-Mode Afterglow of Carbon Dots and Their Applications for Confidential Information Readout. Nano-Micro Letters 13(1) (2021).
